behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description.
behaviors.justification: The core failure boundary is the real model output: even with correct parsing/validation code, the LLM can produce a mismatched signature. A mocked model can mask this by always returning a compliant signature. No OS/network/subprocess behavior is required beyond normal service calls, and this is a single agentic step, not a multi-step pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service against a real LLM with a prompt that specifies a strict typed signature, then assert the generated function signature exactly matches expected parameter names, types, and return type. Repeat across multiple runs/seeds to catch non-deterministic signature drift.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: Whether the implementation actually passes depends on real LLM code quality, which is non-deterministic. Integration tests with canned model outputs can verify loop wiring but cannot catch true pass/fail behavior of model-produced code. This behavior does not inherently require multiple real agentic steps as a minimum, since success may occur on the first call.
behaviors.test_description: Execute end-to-end generation with a real LLM and real test execution for representative tasks; assert the service returns a passing implementation when success occurs on attempt 1 or later attempt, and records which attempt succeeded.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: The failure is at a module handoff boundary: test runner output must be propagated into prompt construction for the next LLM call. This can be caught by wiring several local modules with a fake failing test result and inspecting the next prompt payload. Real LLM behavior is not required to validate inclusion of failure text.
behaviors.test_description: Use a deterministic failing test result fixture, run one failed attempt followed by refinement, and assert the exact failure messages are present in the second-call prompt payload sent by the orchestrator.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily retry orchestration and state aggregation across modules (attempt counter, result collection, response shaping). A mocked always-failing execution component is sufficient to expose real bugs in stop conditions and error structure. Real model quality is not needed to trigger the failure path.
behaviors.test_description: Wire orchestrator + attempt store + result formatter with deterministic failing generation/test outcomes for every attempt; assert exactly 3 attempts occur, no 4th call is made, and response contains all three attempt artifacts and test outputs in the required schema.

behaviors.description: The full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: This explicitly requires multiple real agentic steps in sequence and validates that iterative feedback improves outcomes versus a single call baseline. Mocking would hide the real improvement dynamics, and single-step agentic testing is insufficient because the behavior is about the multi-step loop effect.
behaviors.test_description: Run a benchmark case known to often fail on one-shot generation. Measure one-shot failure, then run the full multi-attempt loop with real LLM calls and real test execution; assert the loop reaches a passing implementation within allowed attempts and preserves intermediate attempt evidence.
